query_theme: data chart graph statistics economy finance

stories.title: Priced Out: How A House Quietly Doubled In Cost
stories.hook: One income bought your parents a house. Not anymore.
stories.closing: Same house, way more money. Welcome to the new rules.

segments.say: A house cost 270 grand in 2019. Today, 449. That extra 180 thousand isn't a nicer place, it's the same house, a whole extra down payment for nothing.
Then rates jumped from 3.9 percent to 6.6. On a 30-year loan that's almost double the interest to the bank, tens of thousands more for the same house.
And renting traps you. In Miami, rent eats 35.7 percent of your income, a third of every check gone before you save a dollar toward buying.
So how long to buy? In San Jose, 11.3 years of your entire salary, every dollar you earn for a decade, just for the house. It feels impossible because it is.

stories.question: Are you ever buying a house? Be honest below.

stories.title: Why Your Grocery Bill Keeps Winning
stories.hook: Same groceries. Way bigger receipt. Where'd it go?
stories.closing: Same cart, fatter receipt. Bon appétit.

segments.say: Since 2020, eggs are up 86 percent. Not 86 cents, 86 percent, while your paycheck barely moved.
And it's still climbing. Beef jumped another 9.2 percent this year, while dairy barely moved at 1.6.

segments.topic: the share of income spent on food
segments.say: Add it up, and food now eats 11.8 percent of your income, the most in decades.
The average bill hit 1,030 dollars a month, about 300 more than 2019. An extra car payment, same fridge.

stories.question: What's gotten insanely expensive at your store? Comment.

stories.title: Americans Owe $1.4 Trillion On Plastic
stories.hook: Americans owe more than ever. It's a trap.
stories.closing: Owe more, save less. The math always wins.

segments.say: Card debt just passed 1,370 billion dollars, nearly 1.4 trillion on plastic, climbing every month.
And interest hit 22.9 percent. At that rate your balance grows even if you never swipe again. That's the trap.
So people fall behind. The share missing payments climbed to 3.9 percent, the most since the last crisis.
Meanwhile savings cratered. In 2020 people saved 16.8 percent of income. Now? 3.6. The cushion is gone.

stories.question: Credit card debt: winning or losing? Tell me below.

stories.title: Your Raise Was Fake (Here's The Math)
stories.hook: You got a raise. So why are you broker?
stories.closing: Bigger paycheck, smaller life. Cool.

segments.say: On paper, a win. The average wage climbed to 31.9 dollars an hour. Your paycheck genuinely got bigger.
But adjust for prices, and the wage index sits at 99, below 2019. After inflation, you earn less than before.
Because the unskippable stuff ran ahead. Childcare is up 31 percent since 2020, while wages rose just 22.

segments.topic: hours of work to cover rent
segments.say: Now it takes 69 hours of work to cover one month's rent, up from 52. Two extra days a month, gone.

stories.question: Did your raise actually help? Comment below.

stories.title: The Jobs Report Hides 3 Ugly Truths
stories.hook: Best job market ever? Then why's everyone broke?
stories.closing: Booming economy. For someone, somewhere.

segments.say: Unemployment sits at just 4.3 percent, historically low. That's the headline. But it hides three things.
First, 4.8 million people are stuck part-time who want full-time. Employed on paper, but not by choice.
Second, participation slid to 62.4 percent. Millions stopped looking entirely, so they vanish from the good number.

segments.topic: people working two or more jobs
segments.say: Third, 9 million people now work two or more jobs, a record. Why does surviving take two paychecks?

stories.question: Is your job market good or brutal right now?

stories.title: Same Salary, Different State, Opposite Life
stories.hook: Same salary. One thrives, one drowns. Why?
stories.closing: Same salary, totally different planet.

segments.say: Cost of living swings wildly. At 184 on the index, Hawaii is about 84 percent pricier than average.
Incomes split too. Massachusetts tops near 96.5 thousand a household, almost double the poorest states.
But adjust for what life costs, and the map flips. A Texas paycheck is worth 78.5 thousand, beating high-cost states.
And rent is the knockout. A one-bedroom in California averages 2,150 dollars, more than double the cheapest states.

stories.question: What state are you in, and is it survivable?

stories.title: You Pay More for Health Insurance and Get Less Every Year
stories.hook: Your plan didn't change. Your bill exploded. Why?
stories.closing: More money in, less protection. That's the deal.

segments.say: Your employer paid 8,120 dollars per worker for health insurance in 2026, up from 6,015 in 2019. That's 35 percent more money for the same coverage window — and that cost shapes what's left over for your raise.

The deductible — what you pay before insurance kicks in — hit 2,340 dollars in 2026. In 2019 it was 1,655. That's 685 extra dollars out of your pocket every year before you're even covered. You're paying more to wait longer.

segments.topic: share with medical debt by income
segments.say: 36 percent of Americans earning under 25 thousand carry medical debt. But even at 50 to 75 thousand, still 18 percent. Medical debt isn't just a poverty problem — it's a system that doesn't protect anyone.
When things go really wrong, you're on the hook for up to 5,620 dollars before insurance pays everything. In 2019, that cap was 3,970. The safety net just got a 1,650 dollar hole in it.

stories.question: What's your deductible this year? Drop it below.

stories.title: The Degree That Tripled in Cost But Pays Half as Much
stories.hook: A degree costs triple. It pays half.
stories.closing: Most expensive ever. Worth less than before.

segments.say: Total student debt hit 1.74 trillion dollars. In 2010 it was 870 billion. The pile doubled in 16 years and keeps growing as a new class graduates every May owing more than the last.
The average monthly student loan payment is 352 dollars. That's higher than most car payments — and it goes to a degree you already have, every single month, for years.

The wage premium for a bachelor's degree fell from 66 percent in 2014 to 54 percent today. The gap between what college grads and high school grads earn is closing. The price went up while the advantage shrank.
And 10.2 percent of federal borrowers are in default. After COVID payment pauses ended, defaults jumped 5 points in one year. The debt is real, and for a lot of people, the math doesn't work.

stories.question: Was your degree worth it? Yes or no below.

stories.title: Most Americans Are Behind on Retirement. Here's How Far.
stories.hook: You're way behind on retirement. Here's how far.
stories.closing: Save more or work forever. Those are basically the options.

segments.say: The median 50-something has 130 thousand saved for retirement. Fidelity says you should have 6 times your salary at 50 — around 360 thousand at median wages. That's a 230,000 dollar hole in the middle of your working life.

Social Security used to replace 41 percent of your pre-retirement income. By 2026, it's down to 31 percent. The gap your savings need to fill just got 10 points wider. But people aren't saving more — they're saving less.

The US personal savings rate hit 3.5 percent in 2026, near a historic low. You can't close a 230,000 dollar gap saving 3 cents on every dollar. The math requires something to change — income, costs, or both.

So what do people do? They work longer. The average retirement age climbed from 60 in 2000 to 66 in 2026. Six extra years of your life because the math didn't work. That's not a choice, that's a system forcing your hand.

stories.question: Are you actually on track to retire? Be honest below.

stories.title: A New Car Costs What a House Used To. Here's the Receipt.
stories.hook: A new car now costs what a house used to.
stories.closing: It's not the avocado toast. It's the car payment.

segments.say: The average new car transaction hit 50,400 dollars in 2026, up from 38,000 in 2019. That extra 12,400 isn't better technology — it's the same truck, just inflated. And it's the number you're financing.

Auto loan rates peaked at 8.1 percent in 2024 — the highest since 2007. On a 50 thousand dollar car over 60 months, that's roughly 10,700 dollars in interest alone. You're buying almost a second car just in interest.
Insurance now costs 2,460 dollars a year on average, more than double the 1,190 it cost in 2019. That's 205 dollars a month just to own the car — before you start it.

Add the payment, insurance, fuel, and maintenance. Total monthly car ownership hit 1,920 dollars in 2026. That's what a 2-bedroom apartment rented for in much of the Midwest not long ago. The car loses value. The lease doesn't.

stories.question: What's your car payment? Drop the number below.

stories.title: We've Found 5,600 Worlds Beyond Our Own
stories.hook: We've found thousands of worlds beyond our own.
stories.closing: Turns out we're not the only address out here.

segments.say: Thirty years ago we knew of zero planets outside our solar system. Today, 5,600 confirmed worlds. Almost all found after 2009, when NASA's Kepler telescope started staring at one patch of sky. One generation, from nothing to thousands.
Most weren't seen, they were inferred. 4,300 were found by the transit method: catching a star dim by a fraction of a percent as a planet crosses it. We never see the planet. We see its shadow.

Most are nothing like home. 2,000 are Neptune-like ice giants and 1,700 are blazing gas giants. Only about 210 are small rocky worlds like Earth. The galaxy is full of planets, just not many you'd want to land on.
And a few are close. Proxima b orbits the nearest star to the Sun, just 4.2 light years away. Close, in cosmic terms. At today's fastest spacecraft, only about 75,000 years to get there.

stories.question: Do you think we're alone out there? Comment below.

stories.title: You're Scared of the Wrong Things. Here's What Kills You.
stories.hook: You're scared of the wrong things. Here's proof.
stories.closing: Sharks are fine. Watch out for the couch.

segments.say: Here's what actually kills Americans. Heart disease takes 695 thousand lives a year. Cancer, 605 thousand. Together, nearly half of all deaths, quiet and slow and almost never breaking news.

segments.topic: annual US deaths from things we fear
segments.say: Now the things that terrify us. Sharks kill about 1 American a year. Lightning, 20. The stuff in your nightmares is a rounding error next to the stuff in your kitchen.
And the real killers are habits. Smoking still takes 480 thousand Americans a year. Bad diet, around 300 thousand. Not accidents or bad luck, slow daily choices that quietly stack the deck.
Add it up and something rare happened. American life expectancy fell, from 78.8 years to 76.4 in just two years. The steepest drop since World War Two.

stories.question: What are you irrationally scared of? Tell me below.

stories.title: Earth Lost Most of Its Wildlife in 50 Years
stories.hook: Earth lost most of its wildlife in one lifetime.
stories.closing: The world went quiet and nobody noticed.

segments.say: Since 1970, the average wildlife population shrank by nearly three-quarters. Scientists track thousands of species in one index, and it fell from 100 to just 27. In a single lifetime, two of every three wild animals, gone.
It's worst in the tropics. Latin America and the Caribbean lost 95 percent of their wildlife. Africa, 76 percent. The richest ecosystems on Earth are emptying the fastest.
Freshwater life is vanishing fastest, down 85 percent. The rivers and lakes you grew up near now hold a fraction of the fish, frogs, and birds they once did.
And it's mostly us. Habitat loss, farms, roads, and cities, threatens 80 percent of at-risk species. Not one villain, just our footprint spreading into everywhere else's home.

stories.question: What animal do you never see anymore? Comment below.

stories.title: You Paid for Inflation Twice — Once at the Store, Once at the Bank
stories.hook: They hiked rates to fix prices. It fixed nothing.
stories.closing: The bill came due. You just didn't know it was yours.

segments.say: Eggs up 44 percent since 2020. Not this year — since 2020. Gasoline up 33, rent up 29, groceries up 25. That's not temporary inflation. That's a new price floor you're living on.
So the Fed hiked. From 0.08 percent in 2021 all the way to 5.33 in 2023. The most aggressive rate cycle since the 1980s. Mortgages, car loans, credit cards — everything got more expensive overnight.
Did it work? Gasoline is still up 28.4 percent year over year. Energy up 17.9. The overall rate is 3.8 — still above target. The stuff you can't avoid is still the most expensive.
And the rate hikes had a cost. The national unemployment rate is 4.3 percent. States that borrowed cheap during the boom are now paying for it. South Dakota stayed at 2.2. Not everyone did.

stories.question: Are higher rates helping or hurting you? Comment.

stories.title: America Got 3 Times Fatter in 30 Years. Here's the Bill.
stories.hook: In 1990, 1 in 8 Americans was obese. Now it's almost 1 in 2.
stories.closing: Bigger country, bigger problem, bigger bill.

segments.say: In 1990, 12 percent of American adults were obese. By 2022, it's 41.9. That's not a gradual drift — it's a generation getting steadily heavier with no sign of stopping.
The US at 41.9 percent isn't just high — it's in another league. Japan is at 4.3. France at 17. Even Mexico and Australia are 12 points lower. We are the global outlier.
Inside the US, it's not even. West Virginia hits 45.3 percent — nearly half the adult population. Mississippi at 44. These states carry the full weight, and their healthcare systems feel every pound.

segments.topic: annual US medical cost of obesity
segments.say: The medical bill? $215 billion a year in 2022 — up from $61 billion in 2000. That's more than the entire defense budget of most countries, every single year, just treating a disease we keep making worse.

stories.question: What changed since the nineties? Your theory below.

stories.title: A Third of America Is Running on Too Little Sleep. Here's What It Costs.
stories.hook: You're awake 16 hours. Your body needed 8 of them to recover.
stories.closing: Half a trillion dollars of tired. And counting.

segments.say: In 1985, 22 percent of adults slept less than 7 hours. By 2024 it's 37 percent. More than 1 in 3 Americans are chronically under-slept, and it's been getting worse every single decade.

segments.topic: average nightly sleep hours by age group
segments.say: Young adults 25-34 average just 6.7 hours — below the CDC's 7-hour minimum. Teenagers get 6.9. Only people 65 and up are actually sleeping enough. The people working hardest sleep the least.

segments.topic: increased health risk from chronic sleep deprivation
segments.say: Sleep under 7 hours raises your obesity risk by 55 percent. Heart disease by 48. Type 2 diabetes by 40. Depression by 55. You're not just tired — you're physically breaking down while you scroll.

segments.topic: annual economic cost of sleep deprivation
segments.say: The RAND Corporation put a dollar on it: $527 billion a year in 2024 in lost productivity and health costs. Sleep-deprived workers miss 11 more workdays a year. You're not just tired — you're expensive.

stories.question: How many hours do you actually sleep? Drop it below.

stories.title: Nobody's Getting Married Anymore. Here's Where It Went.
stories.hook: Your grandparents married at 22. You might not marry at all.
stories.closing: Marriage didn't die. It just got priced out.

segments.say: In 1980, men married at 24.7. Today it's 30.5. Women went from 22 to 28.6. That's not just waiting longer — that's a full decade of your life where you used to be building a family, now you're paying rent alone.
And fewer people are doing it at all. The marriage rate was 10.6 per thousand in 1980. Now it's 5.8 — nearly cut in half. The institution isn't broken, it's becoming a choice fewer people make.
In 1980, 26 percent of 25-to-34-year-olds had never married. Today it's 57 percent. More than half of prime-working-age adults are single. That's not a trend — that's a structural shift.

segments.topic: share of adults married by income quartile
segments.say: And here's the uncomfortable truth: the top income quartile still marries at 58 percent. The bottom quartile? 31. Marriage became a luxury that stability buys. If you can't afford a house, you probably can't afford a wedding either.

stories.question: Married, single, or never? Drop it in the comments.

stories.title: You're Paying for Way More Subscriptions Than You Think
stories.hook: You have way more subscriptions than you think.
stories.closing: Death by a thousand free trials.
stories.question: How many subscriptions are you actually paying for? Comment.

segments.topic: what people think vs really pay
segments.say: Ask people their monthly subscription bill and they guess 86 dollars. The real average is 219. People underestimate by more than half, because every charge is small, automatic, and basically invisible.
Where does 219 dollars go? Video streaming is the biggest slice at 70 dollars a month, then news, cloud storage, gaming, music. No single one feels big. Stacked together, they're a car payment.
And the count keeps climbing. The average household went from 3 subscriptions to 12 in six years. Every app, every service, every free month you forgot to cancel just quietly stacks up.

Here's the business model. 48 percent of people pay for something they don't use. 42 percent forgot they ever signed up. They're not selling you a service anymore. They're selling you forgetting to cancel.

stories.title: We've Mapped Mars Better Than Our Own Ocean
stories.hook: We've mapped Mars better than our own ocean.
stories.closing: The final frontier is under the boat.
stories.question: Would you ride to the bottom of the ocean? Comment below.

segments.say: We have full high-resolution maps of the Moon and Mars. Our own ocean floor? About 25 percent. The least-explored place humans can physically reach is right here on Earth.
We're scrambling to fix it. In 2017 just 6 percent of the seafloor was mapped in detail. By 2023, 25 percent. At this pace the whole ocean floor won't be done until the 2030s.
And it goes deep. A scuba diver tops out near 40 meters. The Titanic rests at 3,800. The Mariana Trench bottoms out near 11,000 meters, deeper than Everest is tall.
It's also full of life we've never named. Scientists have catalogued about 240 thousand marine species and estimate up to 2 million exist. Roughly 9 in 10 ocean species are still a mystery.

stories.title: The Loneliness Epidemic, in Four Charts
stories.hook: More people have zero close friends than ever.
stories.closing: Never more connected. Never more alone.
stories.question: How many close friends do you actually have? Comment.

segments.say: In 1990, 3 percent of Americans said they had no close friends. By 2021 that quadrupled to 12 percent. A growing share of people have literally no one to call when things go wrong.
And we see each other less. In-person time with friends fell from 6.5 hours a week to 2.5. We swapped hangouts for screens, and the screens don't fill the hole.
Here's the gut punch. 30 percent of young adults, 18 to 29, feel lonely often, the highest of any age group. The most digitally connected generation is also the loneliest.
And it's not just sad. Chronic loneliness raises your risk of early death by 26 percent, on par with obesity. Your body treats disconnection like a physical wound.

stories.title: Your Attention Span Collapsed. Here's the Data.
stories.hook: We can't focus for even one minute anymore.
stories.closing: Anyway, you probably already swiped away.
stories.question: Did you make it to the end? Be honest below.

segments.say: A researcher tracked how long people focus on a screen before switching. In 2004 it was 150 seconds. Today, 47. Our default attention span shrank by two-thirds in under 20 years.
Meanwhile we pour fuel on it. Daily short-video time jumped from 20 minutes to 95 in five years. The faster the cuts, the more we crave, the less patience we have left.
We can't sit still either. Gen Z picks up their phone 79 times a day, about once every 12 waking minutes. Every pickup is a reset button on whatever you were doing.
And recovery is brutal. After one interruption it takes 23 minutes to refocus. Stack enough pings and a full hour leaves maybe 40 minutes of real attention. The rest is just getting back on track.
